item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

Event details

item.title: Youth Soapstone Carving Workshops At Best of the Northwest Art Show
item.description:
Saturday, April 11, 2026 at 10:30 AM

Location: Magnuson Park Hangar 30, 6310 NE 74th St., Seattle, WA

The Northwest Stone Sculptors Association \(NWSSA\) is excited to offer Youth Soapstone Carving Workshops during the Northwest Art Alliance Best of the Northwest Spring Show on April 11th & 12th at Magnuson Park Hangar 30. Youth can participate an up-close, hands-on experience with creativity by learning to carve their own soapstone animal figurine. Artist instructors from the Northwest Stone Sculptors Association will guide youth participants Ages 7 – 17 through the process of shaping this beautiful, natural material into a figurine to take home. These workshops are designed to promote fine motor skills, 3D spatial reasoning, and artistic confidence. The soapstone and all necessary kid-safe tools and water to minimize dust will be provided. The youth workshops are Free with admission to the Best of the Northwest Show.   Two 90 minute sessions are available, on Saturday April 11th and Sunday, April 12th beginning at 10:30 am and 1:30 pm.  Spots are limited and registration is encouraged.

Event details

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

Event details

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Sunday, April 12, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

Obstacle Courses & Ninja Training – Agents-in-training will test their agility, speed, and stealth as they navigate exciting ninja courses, crawl through the forest and learn to use nature objects to complete the challenge. Scavenger Hunts & Riddle Solving – Spies must sharpen their detective skills by decoding secret messages, solving riddles, and uncovering hidden clues to complete their top-secret missions. Outdoor Adventure & Camouflage Training – Spies will blend into their surroundings, practice silent movement, and use nature to their advantage while completing their missions. This high-energy camp is perfect for kids who love adventure, problem-solving, and action-packed fun in the great outdoors! Are you ready to accept the mission? - follow the link to book a spot!

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: EMBODIED ALTARS DANCE CLASS
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center, 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA

EMBODIED ALTARS is an all-levels dance class hosted by Daybreak Star Radio. Designed by Indigenous Chicana artist and El Sueño director, Alicia Mullikin. Dancers will utilize Indigenous principles within movement, meditation, and song as a way to practice gratitude, honor the land, and recognize their ancestors. The class is for those who are interested in reflection, intention setting, and a discovery of their own stories. No dance experience necessary. Class Time: Mondays, 6:30-8PM No class 9/15/25 No class 10/13/25 Venue: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA 98199 Sliding Scale: $10-$20\* per class. Sign Up Now!   This venue is ADA accessible w/ ADA bathrooms and a wheelchair lift to the second floor. Class will be held in the ballroom space, this area has 5 ADA parking spaces. There are an additional 25 parking spots in our lot, there is ample parking space along the road leading up to Daybreak star as well as the Discovery Park North Parking lot. What to bring: water, comfortable clothes to dance in, journal, your personal identity, a desire to explore new pathways and an open mind/heart. Hosted at Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center Produced by United Indians of All Tribes Foundation In Partnership with El Sueno Dance & Daybreak Star Radio “Indigenize the Airwaves” “Art. Culture. Reclamation.” \*Talk to us about BIPOC and Queer/LGBTQIA2S\+ Scholorships\* Please contact @el.sueno.dance on IG for private conversations about this topic.

item.title: Man of La Mancha
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 7:30 PM

Location: Theatre Off Jackson, 409 7th Ave S, Seattle, WA

Reboot Theatre Company, in association with Theatre Off Jackson, present an all women/TGNC production of MAN OF LA MANCHA. Directed by Jeannette LeGault, music direction by Aimee Hong , and intimacy and fight choreography by Lee Ann Hittenberger.  Performances run April 3-18, 2026, at Theatre Off Jackson \(409 7th Ave. S. Seattle 98104\) Told through a feminist lens, and performed by an all women/TGNC ensemble, our production of this classic musical explores the ongoing fight for women’s rights, including the pivotal battles of the 21st century. Both urgent and hopeful, this powerful reimagining asks, “What does it mean to dream the impossible dream when your voice, your rights, and your choices are at risk?” Written by Dale Wasserman; Music by Mitch Leigh; Lyrics by Joe Darion; Original production directed by Albert Marre. Man of La Mancha is presented by arrangement with Concord Theatricals on behalf of Tams-Witmark LLC. www.concordtheatricals.com

item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 7:30 PM

Location: Town Hall, 1119 Eighth Avenue (at Seneca Street), Seattle, WA

Join the team behind the popular podcast Planet Money and celebrate the publication of their new book, Planet Money: A Guide to the Economic Forces That Shape Your Life. Book author and longtime contributor Alex Mayyasi, Planet Money host Kenny Malone, and visual host Jack Corbett will bring Planet Money’s signature brand of storytelling to the stage and discuss some highlights from the new book, including unlikely origin stories, revealing truths about human behavior, and insightful suggestions for navigating chaotic times drawn from the latest ideas in economics. Daryl Fairweather, Chief Economist at Redfin, will be the special guest this evening. Come prepared to engage with the silly and the sublime and ask some questions of your favorite Planet Money hosts and reporters. Each book purchased with a ticket comes with an exclusive Planet Money tote bag!

item.title: Gabor Maté
item.description:
Monday, April 13, 2026 at 7:30 PM

Location: McCaw Hall, 321 Mercer Street, Seattle, WA

Embark on a profound journey into the intricate landscapes of the human mind and soul with renowned physician, speaker, and bestselling author, Dr. Gabor Maté. With compassion and unwavering authenticity, Dr. Maté explores the interconnectedness of mind, body, and spirit, shedding light on the underlying causes of human suffering and resilience. Drawing from his groundbreaking research on addiction, trauma, and the impact of early childhood experiences, he offers a transformative perspective on healing and personal growth. Through compelling anecdotes, scientific research, and practical wisdom, Dr. Maté invites audiences to reconsider their understanding of mental health, addiction, and the nature of human connection. Whether you're a healthcare professional, a mental health advocate, or simply a curious seeker of truth, this event promises to inspire, educate, and ignite a deeper understanding of the human condition.

item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Type Design Basics
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Class Overview So you want to design a typeface? Learn how to hand-draw letters and digitize them to create your own unique functional font! In this practical workshop, students will create an original text typeface based on traditional European calligraphy. Whether you want to develop your typography skills, improve your lettering practice, or become a type designer, learning the fundamentals of type design can bring your skills to the next level. Explore both analog media \(pen on paper\) and digital media \(computer\). Learn how to use a calligraphy pen to develop an understanding of the underlying structure of the Latin alphabet. Then you can work on improving your drawings digitally, and create a typeface using Glyphs \(or the font editor of your choice\).   Class time is dedicated to working on your project with individualized feedback, supplemented by lectures, technical demonstrations, and group critique. By the end of the class, you will have a working text typeface of your own creation, as well as a sketch-to-screen workflow that you can use for future font creation. Students will be encouraged to spend up to 10 hours on homework outside of class time. Designing a typeface is a laborious process, but time flies when experimenting, drawing, and editing! This class is offered in partnership with the amazing Seattle Creative Academy \(but this class is not part of their program and is not for credit/degree\)! See also: Experimental Lettering and other Graphic Design or art classes on our website. Learning Objectives Learn the basics of calligraphy Understand the structure and patterning of the Latin alphabet Create your own original letterforms Digitize and refine your original design Learn how to optimize a typeface for text Become familiar with a sketch-to-screen digital type design workflow Who Might Enjoy This Class? Anyone who wants to develop their own custom font Graphic designers, illustrators, lettering artists, type designers -- anyone with related experience who would like to hone their type design skills Architects, engineers, artists, crafters, writers, book lovers -- anyone interested in the form of letters who would enjoy creating and using their own handmade font No prerequisites, but basic graphic design/typography knowledge is recommended

item.title: Write a Feature Film
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Class Overview Do you want to write a screenplay for a feature film, but don’t know where to start? Perhaps you’re a beginner curious about the mechanics of screenplays, or a longtime writer feeling a bit stuck, or a director who would enjoy a new perspective. In this class, gain the skills to turn your ideas into a solid screenplay for a feature film. Gain the tools and building blocks to help guide each part of the writing process for a feature-length screenplay. Learn how to be successful with each step, from idea generation, to writing and revising, and especially outlining and structuring your film. Each 90-minute class will include one or more short in-class exercises, from brainstorming to writing and revising scenes. In the sixth and final class, you will have an opportunity to read an excerpt of your script or present a detailed synopsis to classmates. You’ll be encouraged to work between classes. We’ll look at ways to organize your ideas into an outline that can advance progressively closer to a feature film, discussing diverse and alternative approaches in your structure that might help your script connect with audiences without using clichés. Explore how three-dimensional characters drive plot and how setting, staging, and dialogue can be used to tell your story in an energetic, immersive way. We’ll discuss the differences between writing a screenplay you plan to direct yourself versus one you’re writing for someone else.  There will be handouts and/or reading suggestions offered in class. We’ll look at excerpts from books such as Aristotle’s Poetics and Susan Sontag’s Against Interpretation, as well as film clips and interviews with a diverse array of famous filmmakers across time and space. Get support and guidance on how to find inspiration for your own personal artistic expression and the drive to finish your project. Explore the many joys that can be found in this unique, creative, and multifaceted art form. Learning Objectives Brainstorm and organize ideas for a screenplay Approach structure in a flexible way that allows for different types of films Create three-dimensional characters that form the basis of a story How form can be progressive and new, just like content Find your voice and learn to write comfortably and confidently as yourself Who Might Enjoy This Class? Writers interested in exploring how they might approach a screenplay Anyone who wants to develop their voice as a screenwriter and filmmaker People who have begun a feature-length screenplay and would like the support to continue Artists drawn to other parts of the filmmaking process who would like to explore the writing side Anyone interested in how films are structured and what screenplays are made of Filmmakers who want to create an unconventional piece without sacrificing character and structure No experience is needed; everyone is welcome!

item.title: Chuckle Bucket Tuesday @ Club Comedy Seattle (Open Mic)
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Club Comedy Seattle, 328 15th Ave E, Seattle, WA

Club Comedy Seattle hosts Chuckle Bucket Tuesday each week. The luck of the bucket rules the night. Comics drop their name in and hope to be drawn for one of the spots on the list to tell jokes. It's fun, fast-paced and will keep you laughing hard on your Tuesday night. Door 7:00PM/Show 7:30PM Admission: $7 \+ $0.35 City Of Seattle Admission Tax 21\+ With Valid ID REQUIRED for entry. NO REFUNDS. FYI: If you have a large party it's easier for us to seat you all together if you purchase all the tickets under one name. . Club Comedy Seattle FAQ Is there an age/ID requirement? Yes. We are strictly 21\+ and valid physical ID is REQUIRED For Entry Is Seating First Come/First Served? Yes. The room is all two top tables and we seat guests in the order in which they arrive. Can I reserve a table for a group? We do not take reservations for tables, but groups can arrive early to secure seating together. Are tickets available at the door if an event is sold out online? If an event is sold out online there are no tickets available at the door. What are the club’s hours of operation? The club is open Tuesday to Sunday. Doors open a half hour before each show. What is the Parking like? Generally there is street parking available in the blocks surrounding the club. There are also affordable parking lots on 16th Ave E right around the block from the club along with other easy options in the neighborhood Is there a dress code? There is no formal dress code, but we recommend smart casual attire. Can I bring food or drinks? Outside food and drinks are not allowed. We have a full bar and a menu available for your enjoyment. What if I can't make it and need to cancel my ticket? All ticket sales are final. However, if you can’t make it, please contact us through Eventbrite in advance, and we’ll do our best to accommodate you. Will there be an opener for the headliner? Yes, headliner shows feature a lineup of comedians, including a host, an opener, and the headliner. Will the comics talk to me like I see in online videos all the time? Most likely they won't. Crowd work videos are popular for posting online, but crowd work doesn't generally happen at the online frequency in live comedy show situations. Some comics do engage the crowd as part of their regular set and it's generally always fun and more about making fun of themselves than making fun of the crowd. How can I get on the Cheap Laughs Open Mic On Wednesdays? Submissions for spots on the Wednesday Cheap Laughs Open Mic are accepted only on the Tuesday before between 12:00AM and 11:59PM via email at openmic@clubcomedyseattle.com Do you offer group discounts? Yes, we offer discounts for groups of ten or more. Please contact us for more details. Can I meet the comedians after the show? Meet-and-greet opportunities vary by event. Please ask staff for details after the show.

item.title: Hasan Raheem
item.description:
Tuesday, April 14, 2026 at 7 PM

Location: Neumos, 925 E Pike St, Seattle, WA

Neumos Presents HASAN RAHEEM with Special Guests! TUE, 14 APR 2026 at 07:00PM PDT Ages: All Ages to Enter, 21 & Over to Drink Doors Open: 07:00PM OnSale: Fri, 30 Jan 2026 at 10:00AM PST Announcement: Mon, 26 Jan 2026 at 07:00AM PST Hey, I’m Hasan. I grew up in Gilgit, surrounded by mountains and quiet moments. Eventually, life brought me to Karachi, where I thought I'd just study medicine and figure things out as I went. Honestly, I didn't plan on any of this—music was just something I loved doing after class, a way to unwind, write down feelings, and jam with friends. Fast forward a bit, and now I'm touring the world, playing shows from the US to the UK, and finding myself in places I never imagined. It's genuinely been wild seeing people connect to my songs—something that started out as a personal outlet becoming something bigger, shared, and meaningful. But no matter how many stages I step onto or cities I visit, I'm still that same guy who scribbles lyrics in notebooks, stays up late chasing new melodies, and just tries to keep things real. My sound is a mix of everything—R&B, hip-hop, indie pop—because that's what feels true to me. I love experimenting with different vibes; some days I'll drop something chill, other days I'll create a track that you can't help but dance to. At the end of the day, music for me is about connection—connecting to you, connecting to myself, and telling stories that matter. It's cool knowing we're all experiencing life through music together, each song becoming part of our shared story. Important Information: \* This event is all ages. Valid ID is required to enter bar areas. \* Doors open at the listed event time. \* Event start time is generally one hour after doors open but is subject to change without notice. \* All tickets are nonrefundable with the exception of event cancellation. \* Support acts are subject to change. \* Orders in violation of the published ticket limit are subject to cancellation without notice.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

item.title: SP 101: Intro to Vera's Screen Print Studio
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Book-It Repertory Theatre, 305 Harrison Street, Seattle, WA

This 2-hour, comprehensive hands-on class teaches you all the basics of screen printing at VERA’s screen printing studio: From hand-drawing a stencil on transparency, coating and burning a screen, to pulling your very first print! Get comfortable with Vera's shop layout, equipment, and culture. Learn techniques for creating screens, terminology, and more to create 1-color projects in the print shop. Students will walk away with the basic skills for screen printing 1-color paper prints, and their own print of a special Vera design, specific to the 101 class. This class is a prerequisite for 200-level classes at VERA, as well as access to Open Sessions to work on your own projects in VERA’s screen printing studio.

item.title: Tai Chi for Health and Balance
item.description:
Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 6 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Class Overview Tai Chi is a health sustaining exercise and a tool for improved body awareness. Practiced by the elderly for balance and circulation and the young for relaxation and martial arts, it is a wonderful holistic activity for all ages. We'll focus on learning the entire Yang short form, and you'll receive guidance in developing your own regular practice at home. During this class, you'll learn: -The Yang Tai Chi short form, which is comprised of 35 individual movements -Chi Kung, a warm-up exercise and relaxation activity with 18 movements -Elements of Chinese Medical Philosophy, such as Yin/Yang theory and Five Elements transformation -the basics of Taoist philosophy Learning Objectives Learn basic warm-up stretches to improve your overall movement Discover the Yang-style short form of Tai Chi Practice the Yang-style under the direction of an expert instructor Gain knowledge about the history and background of Tai Chi Who Might Enjoy This Class? Individuals seeking a low-impact exercise routine Anyone interested in improving their coordination and balance People interested in developing their focus, relaxation and overall wellness

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Moomins' Sea Adventures & Tove and the Sea
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: National Nordic Museum, 2655 NW Market Street, Seattle, WA

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

item.title: Contemporary Dance for Every Body
item.description:
Thursday, April 16, 2026 at 4 PM

Location: Seattle Central College Continuing Education, 1701 Broadway, Seattle, WA

Class Overview Would you like to cultivate body awareness through movement? Explore a grounded and natural approach to moving your body, led by a contemporary dance instructor. Through floorwork, movement sequences, and phrasing, this class cultivates body awareness. Explore efficiency in movement transitions. Develop a deeper understanding of how your body moves naturally in relation to the floor, emphasizing organic flow, continuity, spirality, softness, core engagement, and the intelligent use of gravity. Open your mind and body through guided movement! Explore points of support, releasing tension, and engaging the core to facilitate flow and prevent injury. Movement principles will draw from contemporary dance and somatic practices, encouraging dancers to move with clarity, sensitivity, and presence. This class is for dancers, actors, physical theater artists, yoga/movement practitioners, and anyone interested in exploring movement in their bodies -- no experience necessary. Designed to be inclusive, this class offers both accessibility and challenge to movers at various levels and all backgrounds.  Learning Objectives Understand and embody key principles of movement floorwork, including gravity, spirality, and core initiation. Improve movement continuity and flow between levels \(floor to standing and vice versa\). Explore softness and efficiency through core engagement. Identify and use anatomical points of support for safe and fluid movement. Develop new dance tools Feel more connected with the possibilities of your body in a conscious way to live a more present, healthy, and aware life. Who Might Enjoy This Class? Adult learners of all backgrounds \(18\+\), including dancers, actors, physical theater artists, yoga/movement practitioners Anyone interested in embodied movement practices. Some prior movement or dance experience is helpful but not required. The class will be structured to accommodate varying levels of experience and physical ability.

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

item.title: Springing Forward: Brunch for Black Economic Brilliance
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Amazon Frontier, 2205 7th Ave, Seattle, WA

Experience the Central District Community Preservation and Development Authority \(CDCPDA\) benefit brunch: Springing Forward: Brunch for Black Economic Brilliance. Now in its second year, this annual gathering brings together more than 200 visionaries—professionals, philanthropists, policymakers, and community leaders—united by a shared commitment to advancing Black economic empowerment in Seattle’s Central District and beyond.  Anchored in the themes: Collaborate. Sustain. Thrive. The brunch creates a dynamic space for meaningful connection, shared learning, and forward-looking action. The program will highlight current tenants, key partnerships, and community activations, while also providing important updates on the progress of the McKinney Center for Community and Economic Development—and a look ahead at what’s to come for the future of this vital project. Guests will engage directly with CDCPDA staff and board members, as well as tenants of the McKinney Center, a cornerstone community hub driving inclusive economic growth. In partnership with Amazon’s Black Employee Network, this event moves beyond celebration to the intentional cultivation of Black excellence and long-term community sustainability. Join us for a meaningful experience that goes beyond traditional networking—one rooted in collaboration, resilience, and collective progress. Your presence helps fuel transformative impact and strengthens the foundation for a thriving, equitable future. Attendee | $150.00﻿ ﻿Full access to the brunch, keynote, and networking. Your ticket directly supports the McKinney Center and community-rooted development in the Central District. Vendor Table | $175.00 Table your business or organization during the first hour of the event. Includes one attendee ticket. Full Table \(8 seats\) | $1,200.00 Bring your team or clients. Includes 8 individual tickets — a savings of $1,000 vs. individual pricing. Guest details can be submitted after purchase. Be part of the movement. Together, we spring forward—collaborating, sustaining, and thriving. Our Emcee TraeAnna Holiday, a true Seattleite, has watched her city change in many ways. She started training in media from the young age of 9 and continued through school up to Howard University and University of Washington. Through studying abroad multiple times, gaining her degree in Communications and Urban Studies, and being displaced by gentrification, she's now fueled to tell her neighborhood's stories through film and media. She found a home for this work at Converge Media as a two-time Emmy award winning Media Personality, TV Show Host and Producer, with her daily talk show, The Day With Trae, and other productions. As Communications Director for Washington state’s Office of Equity, she is able to bring her knowledge and enthusiasm for communications and the community to the state level. Flourishing with her style, passion, and drive, she dynamically creates impact media throughout her work, and helps the next generation find their voice in the world of media.  Our Keynote Speaker LaNesha McCoy is a skilled public historian with over 20 years experience leading African American museums and history initiatives. She is Executive Director of BlackPast.org, the world’s largest online encyclopedia on Black history which was founded by Dr. Quintard Taylor. Prior, she served as President & CEO of the Northwest African American Museum from 2017 to 2023 where she led strategic planning, recordsetting fundraising, and innovative programming. LaNesha is Past National President of the Association of African American Museums Board of Directors, only the sixth woman in history to serve in that capacity. A member of the International Council of Museums, she has studied museums globally in Kenya, Ghana, South Africa, England, Germany, and Israel. An ordained clergy, LaNesha is Assistant Pastor at the historic New Hope Missionary Baptist Church in Seattle's Central District and Executive Administrator of the New Hope Community Development Institute, which is focused on building affordable housing for people of color. Never forgetting her roots or the many mentors that shaped her life, she now has three master's degrees and is currently a PhD Candidate at the University of Washington where she teaches and researches African American educational history. She's a mentor and advocate, leading initiatives to support education.

item.title: Decay
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: The Vestibule, 5919 15th Ave. N.W., Seattle, WA

Decay explores how we navigate and engage with the inevitable entropy of our world. This exhibition shows us the joy of degeneration, the beauty of collapse, the peace that comes from crumbling into dust. The work, primarily from Pacific Northwest artists, ranges from sculptural, to wall-mounted 3D, video and installation works. Included in this exhibition will be works created using natural and reclaimed materials, as well as pieces designed to create spaces for quiet reflection on the theme. Decay appears as part of the natural world and in our bodies in many forms, and so over the duration of the show, some works may change over time, exemplifying the theme. Many of the pieces prompt reflection on mortality, the fragility of the physical body and the sacred.

Each work is an exploration of the vivid, sensory experience of sound translating into vibrant, emotional visuals. As an artist with synesthesia each work expresses Scott’s personal perception of what music looks like. Scott uses resin techniques that he has pioneered and pushes the boundaries of this medium.

item.title: Forterra Fridays: April 2026
item.description:
Friday, April 17, 2026 at 1 PM

Location: Duwamish Hill Preserve, 3800 South 115th Street, Tukwila, WA

Forterra Fridays: April 2026 Friday, Apr. 17, 2026, 1:00 pm to 3:30 pm We’re excited to invite you to volunteer with us for a special Earth Month event at Duwamish Hill Preserve, a Tukwila park providing critical wildlife habitat and public trails in the middle of an industrial area. Join us on Friday, April 17th, from 1:00 pm to 3:30 pm, to restore essential wildlife habitat by removing Himalayan blackberries that threaten our local native trees, shrubs, and groundcover. Established native plants benefit local wildlife, improve water quality, and enhance air quality.  You will also gain valuable insights into ecological restoration and our innovative approach to land conservation. We’ll start with a walk of the site and Earth Day-themed trivia and have restoration professionals on hand to answer questions about plants, restoration, and the Green Tukwila Partnership and Forterra.  What we are doing: The Preserve now includes an outdoor classroom area, the Cultural Garden, an enhanced trail system, and continued restoration opportunities for volunteers. We’ll be removing the blackberry to ensure the trees, shrubs, and groundcover plants can thrive.   It’s a great opportunity to learn about Forterra’s innovative approach to land conservation, including its role in safeguarding the Duwamish Hill Preserve.  What to bring: Please dress for the weather \(in layers to stay warm/dry\). Snacks, drinks, tools, and gloves will all be provided. Where to park: Park in the nearby parking lot at Duwamish Hill Preserve. Signs direct you from the parking lot to where we will meet.  Date and Time: Friday, April 17, 2026, 1:00 –3:30 PM  Register and learn more here.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Green Issaquah: Restoration Event at Park Pointe
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 9 AM

Location: SE Evans St, Issaquah, WA

Join the Issaquah Alps Trails Club and Green Issaquah on the third Saturday of each month for invasive species removal events at Park Pointe! We will be removing invasive species such as Himalayan blackberry bushes to prepare for our fall planting event. These invasive plants inhibit the growth of native plants, and removing them will ensure the longevity and health of our newest additions. This event is part of our ongoing efforts to help meet the Issaquah Climate Action Plan goal of 55% tree canopy cover by 2035. When and Where Date & Time: Saturday, April 18th, 2025, 9 AM - Noon Where: Park Pointe Where to Park Parking for Park Pointe is available along SE Evans Street, which is overflow for Issaquah High School and the parking for the Sportsman Club. Where to Meet We will be meeting at the end of the dirt road at the trailhead. What to Bring Bring your own gloves if you have them. There will be gloves available for those who don't have their own. Please dress in layers and in clothing you don't mind getting dirty. Closed-toed shoes are required. Long sleeves and pants, especially denim, are strongly recommended. Bring a full water bottle and snacks. Don't forget to bring your sunscreen! Point of Contact Anne Newcomb \(anne@annenewcomb.net\) and Matt LaBelle \(mattlabelle@hotmail.com\), your work party leaders.

item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Dive into the whimsical world of Moominvalley and the life of its creator, Tove Jansson, with *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* and *Tove and the Sea*. *Moomins’ Sea Adventures* brings Moominvalley to life with illustrations and life-sized characters from the stories. In the center of the exhibit is an interactive island complete with a lighthouse and rowboats that invites visitors to imagine adventures of their own. *Tove and the Sea* offers a more intimate counterpoint, tracing Tove Jansson’s lifelong relationship with the Finnish archipelago through large-scale photographs and reflective texts.

item.title: South Lynnwood Park Stewardship Event
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Scriber Lake Park, 5562 196th St SW, Lynnwood, WA

City of Lynnwood Parks welcomes volunteers of all abilities who want to contribute to the restoration and conservation of our parks in South Lynnwood Neighborhood. The goal of each event is to provide a safe, enjoyable, and effective hands-on experience for you to help improve the health of your urban parks. These monthly events, which will be held on the third weekend of each month, will involve removing invasive plants, help with restoration plantings, and picking up garbage. Events are 100% outdoors and will generally be held ‘rain or shine’ but may be canceled or relocated if weather or site conditions are unsafe such as high winds, flooding, snow, lightning, or unsafe air quality \(smoke\).

Event details

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Woodinville Market Fair: Spring April event
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Avallé Winemaking Studio & Watts Brewing Co, 15500 Woodinville Redmond Rd NE, Woodinville, WA

Spring is in the air, Seahawks spirit is everywhere, and the Woodinville Market Fair is bursting back to life! Join us on April 18th as we kick off a brand‑new season filled with incredible local vendors, delicious food trucks, raffle prizes, and all the warm, welcoming energy that makes our community shine. It’s the perfect way to celebrate fresh beginnings and the start of another unforgettable Woodinville Market Fair season. Both of our locations are family‑friendly spaces, perfect for spending a spring day together and celebrating fresh beginnings.Vendors interested in participating — we’d love to have you! Send us a message for details on how to join the lineup.

Event details

item.title: Earth Month Event | Restoration at Homestead Park
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Join us in Homestead Park where we will be removing common ivy and blackberry crowding native plants. This will be a great opportunity to learn the basics of restoration and plant identification, all while making our local forests healthier. Come get your hands in the dirt, work alongside neighbors and friends, and eat snacks! No experience necessary all ages welcome; gloves and tools are provided. Please note: we require participants aged 14 and under to volunteer with a parent or guardian at a ratio of 1 adult to 3 kids. When: Saturday, April 18th, 10am-1pm Where: Homestead Field, follow VOLUNTEER EVENT signs What to bring: Dress for the weather in clothes you don't mind getting dirty \(think: long p ants, boots or sneakers\). Bring a water bottle. Small snacks, gloves, and tools will be provided. Registration: https://volunteersignup.org/RMPY L

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Saturday, April 18, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details
